standard_description: Interpret products of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 5 × 7 as the total number of objects in 5 groups of 7 objects each.
sources: Carpenter, T.P., Fennema, E., Franke, M.L., Levi, L., & Empson, S.B. (2014). Children's Mathematics: Cognitively Guided Instruction (2nd ed.). Heinemann.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Operations and Algebraic Thinking (OA) K–5, 2011
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic A: Multiplication and the Meaning of the Factors, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Adds the two numbers instead of multiplying
misconceptions.description: When asked 'How many in all?' for equal groups, the student adds the number of groups to the number in each group rather than multiplying. For '3 groups of 4', the student computes 3+4=7 instead of 3×4=12.
misconceptions.example: 3 bags, 4 apples each. Student answers 7 (3+4) instead of 12 (3×4 or 4+4+4).
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) p.50 — 'A persistent early error is the additive interpretation of multiplicative situations: the student adds the two given numbers, treating the problem as a join rather than as repeated equal groups.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use physical manipulatives: place 3 plates with 4 counters on each. Have the student count all counters. Compare to 3+4=7 by physically pushing 3 and 4 counters together. Emphasize: 'Equal groups means the same number REPEATED, not just added once.'

misconceptions.short_label: Confusing number of groups with size of each group
misconceptions.description: Student reverses the meaning of 'groups of'. For '5 boxes with 4 pencils in each', the student thinks there are 4 boxes of 5 pencils. Although 5×4=4×5 numerically, the student's MODEL of the situation is wrong, leading to errors in word problems where direction matters.
misconceptions.example: 'Each box has 4 pencils, 5 boxes total.' Student draws 4 boxes with 5 pencils each instead of 5 boxes with 4 pencils each. May still get 20 by accident, but cannot answer follow-up questions like 'How many boxes?'
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions K-5 (2011) p.23 — 'Students often invert the role of the multiplier and multiplicand, especially when the problem statement places the size of the group before the number of groups; explicit attention to which number names the GROUPS and which names the SIZE is essential.'
misconceptions.remediation: Underline the GROUP word (boxes, plates, bags) and circle the SIZE word (pencils, cookies, apples). Say aloud: 'There are ___ groups, and each group has ___.' Always draw the groups first as containers, then fill in the size.

misconceptions.short_label: Counts only the groups OR only the items, not both
misconceptions.description: Student reports just the number of groups (e.g., 5) or just the size of one group (e.g., 4) instead of the total. The multiplication step is skipped entirely — the student stops after identifying one of the two factors.
misconceptions.example: 5 plates with 3 cookies each. Student answers 5 (number of plates) or 3 (cookies per plate) instead of 15.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.156 — 'Some students respond to multiplication situations by reporting one of the factors, indicating that they have not yet built the conceptual link between equal groups and the iterated total.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Ask 'How many groups?' and 'How many in each group?' as two separate questions. Then say: 'Now count ALL the items together.' Use skip counting aloud while pointing at each group.

misconceptions.short_label: Skip counts the wrong number of times
misconceptions.description: Student skip counts correctly by the group size but stops too early or too late. For '4 groups of 5', student counts '5, 10, 15' (only 3 skips) and answers 15, or counts '5, 10, 15, 20, 25' (5 skips) and answers 25.
misconceptions.example: 3 groups of 4. Student counts '4, 8, 12, 16' (4 skips) and answers 16 instead of 12.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Topic A Teacher Edition — 'A common skip-count error is misalignment between the number of skips and the number of groups; students should physically tap each group while skip counting to enforce the one-to-one correspondence.'
misconceptions.remediation: Have student tap each group (or each finger) while skip counting. Say: 'Skip count exactly as many times as there are groups — no more, no less.' Use a number line with arcs of equal size to make each skip visible.

misconceptions.description: Student does not check whether all groups have the same number of items before multiplying. Given groups of [3, 3, 2], the student multiplies 3×3=9 (assuming all are 3) or skip counts incorrectly. The 'equal' condition is overlooked.
misconceptions.example: Picture shows 3 plates: 4 cookies, 4 cookies, 3 cookies. Student says '3 groups of 4 = 12' instead of counting 4+4+3=11.
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) p.52 — 'The conceptual prerequisite for multiplication — that all groups must contain the same number — is often assumed by students without verification, leading to errors when problems present near-equal but not exactly equal groups.'
misconceptions.remediation: Before multiplying, ask: 'Are all the groups the same size?' If not, instruct the student to add (not multiply) or to redistribute the items first. Use mixed equal/unequal example sets to train discrimination.

misconceptions.short_label: Reports the size of one group as the total
misconceptions.description: Student finds the size of one group correctly but reports it as the answer for 'how many in all?' This is a comprehension error — the student thinks 'each' refers to the total rather than to one unit.
misconceptions.example: '5 sandwiches, each has 2 slices of cheese. How many slices in all?' Student answers 2 (each sandwich's count) instead of 10 (total).
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.157 — 'The word "each" causes confusion: some students interpret "2 slices each" as the total count rather than as the per-unit count, particularly when the problem is presented orally or with minimal pictorial support.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Translate 'each' to 'every one of': '2 slices on every sandwich' and then ask 'How many sandwiches? How many slices on EACH? How many slices on ALL of them together?' Use bar models to separate per-unit vs total.

misconceptions.short_label: Skip counts by groups instead of by group size
misconceptions.description: Student skip counts by the number of groups rather than by the size of each group. For '4 groups of 5', the student counts '4, 8, 12, 16, 20' (skip count by 4, five times) instead of '5, 10, 15, 20'. Although the answer may coincide, the model is wrong and fails when factors differ in scale.
misconceptions.example: 3 groups of 6. Student skip counts '3, 6, 9, 12, 15, 18' (six skips of 3) instead of '6, 12, 18' (three skips of 6). Final answer 18 is correct by coincidence (commutative), but cannot reason about 'how many groups' if asked.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions K-5 (2011) p.24 — 'Skip counting must use the SIZE of each equal group as the count-by unit, with the number of skips equal to the number of groups; reversing this leads to a faulty mental model even when the numerical answer is correct.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always identify SIZE-of-group first: 'How many in each? That's our skip-count number.' Then identify number of GROUPS: 'That's how many times we skip.' Practice with non-square examples (e.g., 2 groups of 7) where the two strategies give different intermediate counts.
